speaker: interviewer
text: June 7, 1995. Survivor Norbert Friedman. Interviewer, Mark Goldberg. We're in West Hempstead, New York in the United States. The language is English. Please continue telling me about Leonberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing that's significant in my life-- Leonberg-- in the winter of 1944, either it was November or December, they brought a transport from Gross-Rosen, I think. And some of the prisoners were missing. And usually when somebody was missing, they lined you up, did the count, did the search. And here we were standing in rows at attention with our hats off. And the transport that they just brought was standing about 40, 50 feet away from us. There was a young fellow about my age standing there, who looked familiar, but not quite. And he looked at me also with some kind of sign of recognition, but we could not get to each other because we had to stay at attention. And this took several hours. But at the time when the counting was over, we were told to put our hats on, [GERMAN]. We put our hats on, we recognized each other. The reason was we have never seen each other with our heads shaven, in such a state. This was my childhood friend. From the time I was eight years old, from fourth grade on, we were very close friends. We went to school together, and we walked home from school together. We belonged to the same Zionist organization. We shared many dreams and many discussions. We were very close to each other. And I lost track of him when the war broke out. And here he was. And from that time on, we stayed together. Sometime, beginning of 1945, when the American troops began to try to get through the river into German-- I forgot the name of the river, it's immaterial. But we could hear the cannons and so on. The camp was being liquidated. We were called together in the middle of the night. And they issued us a ration of boiled cabbage and a piece of bread. That cabbage was not that well boiled. And we were walking from the camp-- in the middle of the night-- to an unknown destination. That particular ration of cabbage caused diarrhea to myself and to many people. And here we were walking in the transport. And there was a terrible dilemma, because in order to relieve yourself, you had to ask permission to step out from the marching. And if the convoy passed you, then they shot you. Because they wouldn't leave any stragglers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what you do, you used to run, try to wiggle into the front of the convoy, ask permission, and then relieve yourself, so that the transport would not pass you by. It was a terrible feeling of suffering dysentery on an empty stomach and marching. We marched for-- I don't know-- about six or eight hours. You said 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: The beginning of 1945. This is maybe January of '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're not in Leonberg anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Leonberg was being liquidated because of the advancing American army.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The American army was close to the-- they were about to cross the river and march into German territory. And they liquidated the camp. And they were taking us out. And they marched us for a long time towards a railroad siding, where they're going to put us on cars and take us away. And I was advised not to eat the bread. Because if I ate the bread, it would just go right through me. It would be of no benefit to me. And so I kept the bread in the side of my pocket. At one point while we were marching, an SS guard told me and another man to pick up a man who was fainting by the roadside. This happened in a-- we were marching through the village. Apparently, they didn't want to shoot anybody while we were going through the village. They didn't want people to know what they were doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we picked up this man. And he was already white. And he was just about to expire. And we carried him in the blanket. I held where his feet were, and my friend was holding the front.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that man was just about to die, and he spotted the bulge in my pocket. And he probably realized it was bread. And in this very weak voice-- he said to the SS men-- he says that I stole his ration of bread. That's his piece of bread that I'm having. And the SS man lashed out and hit me across the face, bloodied my nose, and made me give the man my portion of bread. I didn't argue, and I gave him as we carried him, And after about a half an hour, the man expired. And we laid him down. And I went down, tried to extract my portion of bread from a dead person. In his hands, he convulsively held, even after he was dead, I had to break this portion of bread to take out of his hands. And this probably was one of the lowest times of my life. Because under Jewish law, it is forbidden. Anything that comes in contact with a dead person is dirty, is not clean. And here I was-- in order to fill my stomach, I took something that a dead person held. It was my own ration bread. I took it from him. And when we got to the railroad sidings, it was bombed out. And there were fires. My father told me to put the bread in the fire-- and it burned into charcoal-- and then eat the charcoal. This is how I stopped my dysentery. We got on the transport. And we were brought to a place. And it was night. And we had to lay down in the meadow. And it was pouring terribly. And we had to lay in the water. The water was coming up. We had difficulties keeping our head above the water. And if you got up, they shot you. And we laid there for the rest of the night, submerged completely in the water-- just our heads-- most of us. Some of them had better positions. They were a little better-- on the higher grounds. And then they took us [GERMAN]. There was the Camp Kaufering. And Camp Kaufering, for the first time since we left Poland, we saw women. But they were in another barracks, about 300 feet away. And we could see women walking, their heads shaven, in the long, emaciated. And we wanted so much to get close to them. Because we thought maybe there's some members of our family, maybe the mothers or sisters or spouses. And we just couldn't get to it. Camp Kaufering, the internal administration was run by the Russian POWs. And they were thieves. They stole everything. They stole your shoes, whatever there was-- even the belts or even a piece of rope that tied your clothes up. In Kaufering we were not very long. From there, we went to Camp Natzweiler. And from Natzweiler, we went to Ganacker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you talk about Ganacker, you said something interesting when you were talking about Kaufering and the women. At this point, now it's 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: You haven't seen your mother, brother, members of your family in three years. How certain were you-- you said you wanted to look for relatives. How certain were you that your family was gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were certain that they were gone. But yet there was hope against hope, in the back of our minds, maybe. Odds against odds-- we didn't know the extent to which the exterminations were taking place. We had no idea that Belzec was created specifically for extermination, that they exterminated over 600,000-- only Jews. Only Jews. 600,000 Jews were killed, gassed in Belzec. Only two people survived that camp. One of them went back to his home in Lublin, and the Poles killed him. And the other one expired several years ago in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I mean, by that time, we didn't know. We know they took them to a camp. I mean, something bad happened. But maybe they ran away. Maybe somebody made it.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were young women in our family. So I mean, maybe they made it. Maybe somebody that they knew-- maybe somebody knew how they died. There was always hope. There was this thread of hope in your mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Back to Ganacker.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came to the Camp Ganacker. Camp Ganacker was several miles away from an airfield where the Germans had their squadron of Messerschmitt 262. We would march every morning to work on the airfields, do whatever digging, whatever else was necessary. On April 13, the guards informed us that President Roosevelt was dead. President Roosevelt died April 12, 1945. By the time the news got there, over there it was already April 13 in the morning. And they said, your friend is dead. The war is over. You are finished. And they took whatever last bits of hope we have. That very day at noon, the American planes came in-- I think the P-47s. They dived. They picked out the German planes-- the Messerschmitts-- that were parked against the barns and barracks, in order not to be picked up. They strafed and they destroyed them. And miraculously, they were able to pick up-- the SS was out machine gun-- the SS guards were in the perimeter-- without hurting any of the prisoners. When we saw the planes on fire and the guards running, we ran. They had mounds of potatoes and kohlrabies that they kept in the mounds for the winter. We went over there, dug them out, pulled them out, and we-- and the burning planes among exploding. Machine gun ammunitions, we begged them. And then we danced around the planes. Because this was a dance of defiance. That was for the first time we saw the beginning of the end for the Germans. Camp Ganacker, the conditions were very bad. We lived in hovels, dug out into the ground. And the place where we slept was a little bit elevated from the ground. The few stalls, and that's where we slept. It was wet and cold all the time over there. The guards were very vicious. I know that in the mornings after the rain, when the dew was in the field, some of the French prisoners from Luxembourg used to jump out from the line. They were looking for snails. And the guards-- when they used to bend down-- they used to aim at their testicles. And they'd shoot them just out for fun, just out of pure viciousness. On April 23, in the middle of the night, we were awakened. We were told to assemble, told to take our blankets. And we were being taken to an unknown destination. Weeks before that, we could hear the American planes bombing the adjacent cities, the ground heaving. The Americans flew during the day. And at night, the British used to come and bomb. And at that time in April, we could hear heavy artillery guns from far. We were taken on a march. There was about 2,700 people that started out on the march. On the third day of the march, there was only about 900 of us left. 1,800 were left in the ditches alongside the road. It was about a 60-mile stretch that we covered in those three days. My friend who was with me-- his name was Oscar Klausenstock, and now presently Dr. Oscar Klausenstock-- developed a temperature. And he said he wasn't going to make it anymore. He couldn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at one of the stops, my father said, I should tell you what. People were laying down and dying. He says, lay down, and we'll cover you as if you were dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we covered him with branches, and he laid in the ditch. And the convoy marched on. He was left over there. On the 26th of April, the third day of the march, the SS major of the convoy and the eldest of the prisoners passed by the convoy on a motorcycle. There was a bolt in it. And they went. They left, and they came back about a half an hour later. And they took us to a clearing. They announced that we're surrounded by American troops, and each and every SS man should take about five or six prisoners and go to find a hiding place. And when the glorious German army will conquer the Americans, we will reassemble. What it was, it was a ruse that was planned between the SS major and the camp commandant, the head of the prisoners. In exchange for saving the lives of those people, the eldest of the prisoners promised to vouch for the camp commandant when the Americans will liberate. When the SS heard that, they started disappearing into the woods. And we ran into the night. We eventually found ourselves in a barn in the outskirts of a village. And we went into this particular barn. But before we went, we took with us one SS man, who was really not an SS man. We watched the SS. We knew who was mean, who was bad, who was good. This was an old Hungarian, ethnic German, who, when the Germans went into Hungary, they put him into the Wehrmacht first. They put him first into the whatever they would call the land army and then to the Wehrmacht. That man, he couldn't even speak German. He never hurt anybody, never harmed anybody. So we took him with us. And we were in the barn. And he used to go out and get food for us. And we stayed there. On May 1-- it was strange. May 1, in the morning, when we woke up, it snowed. It was already spring, it was warm already. But May 1, it snowed. The whole landscape was covered in snow, as if nature wanted to cover out the blood-soaken earth, put a mantle of innocence on it. And we heard rumblings. And when we looked through the barn. And we saw tanks with five-pointed stars on them and soldiers next to it, marching through the road. At first, we thought these were Russians, because to us a five-pointed star would indicate Russians. But when we looked at the soldiers, somebody says, no, no, they're Americans. They're Americans. And they went into the village. About noon, a decision was made by my father and several friends-- we were together in that group-- that I should go to the village and find out what's the story. The reason was that they knew that I loved American movies before the war. And I knew several expressions in English, such as, goodbye, I love you. And that, according to their perception, should have been enough for me to communicate with the Americans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here I walked on the cobblestones of the Bavarian village, looking out behind my shoulder in disbelief, because it was the first time in many, many years that I walked without a guard, free and unable to perceive and to appraise the fact that I was free. And I marched. And I still can hear the sound of my clogs hitting the cobblestones of the road. And my feet were lacerated and swollen and bleeding.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here I come into the village square. And the American soldiers sitting on their helmets, leaning against the walls of the thing. And the armored vehicles parked outside the square. And here I walked-- this 90-pound apparition, emaciated, bewildered-- and I walked into this square. And most of the soldiers were in the process of eating some food. And everything stopped. Everything was like slow motion. And I turned around, bewildered, looking around. I didn't know how to assess the situation. I didn't know who they were, and they didn't know who I was. And one of them beckoned, yelled to me in broken German-- probably from an American army handbook-- [GERMAN]. And I walked over to him. And he asked me, and I tried to explain. I didn't dare to say I was Jewish. I said I was Polish. The fear of being a Jew, the persecution and everything was still in me. And I didn't dare to admit. I said I'm Polish. And he called a soldier who spoke Polish and came over. And he asked me who I was, what was I. And I told him more or less. And they started putting food, care rations and C-rations in my arms. And I couldn't hold it. And the packages were falling to the ground, and I was losing. And the tears were running down my cheeks. Because here there was life-giving bounty, and I couldn't hold onto it. And they picked it up. And they put it in a sack. And they gave it to me. And I went back to my friends. And I told my father about this army that sits on helmets and eats Napoleons. Because they were eating the C-rations, crackers with processed cheese in between. And I thought these were Napoleons. I thought these were custard cakes or something, I said, these are unbelievable. And I showed them what they gave me. That company stayed for a couple of days. A few days later, two six-by-six trucks passed by the road, not far from where we were. And there was a little river. There was a bank, because there was a mill. The farmer had a mill. And he used to power it from the water. He backed up river. There were four black American soldiers. They threw hand grenades for the fish to come out. And they cut the fish, and they made the bonfire. And they were making themselves a meal outside. And my father said, go and bring them in. I said, they're eating outside. We were living that time already in the farmhouse. Thanks to my father, we were still alive. Because many, many of our people were dying from overeating. Those that survived died after liberation because their system could not absorb the food, could not process the food. And some of them were in the process of dying. But some of them couldn't absorb the food. And they were dying from dysentery and other internal disorders. My father, who had experience from World War I, for the first couple of days would only let us eat boiled water with milk and bread broken into it. And then later killed a chicken, and some chicken soup. And here were these black GIs, sitting outside, eating outside. And he says, bring them in the house. So I went over to them. And I couldn't communicate with them very well. And they were sitting, and they were eating fish and some of the food from K-rations. And I come back. I said, daddy, you don't want these people. I said, they're [GERMAN]. They're wild people. They're eating fish with marmalade. Something we had never seen. They had cranberry sauce. I didn't know what it was. He said, never mind. Go, and I went and I brought them in. I brought these four black soldiers into the farmhouse. One of them was a mime. I mean, at least he could communicate with us by miming. He was unbelievably talented. And he told us a little about whatever he could tell us, about life in the United States. And they had a meal with us. And they stayed overnight. This particular gentleman, his name was Norman C. Norman. He was from Detroit. Slept in the same room with me. When he was undressing at night, at the light of the moon, I could see his shining black body. I never saw a black person before in my life. He had the BVDs, the white shorts. And he went to bed. And he got up in the morning before I woke up. And after he left, I got up and I checked the bed. I was sure there had to be some black stains on his sheets, that something had to come off from his shiny black body. And they left. About two or three days later, two of those soldiers-- he wasn't among them-- came with a truckload of food. They worked in a transportation company. And whatever they could organize, [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] they loaded a six-by-six truck. And they pulled it into the barn, and they unloaded. And there was C-ration, K-ration cigarettes. Among other things, there was chocolate. I hadn't seen chocolate in six years.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was this big block of cooking chocolate. I didn't know what it was. I hid it for myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I ate it. And I was constipated for four weeks. And I don't eat chocolate anymore. [LAUGHS] But that act of kindness was the seed of my personal relationship towards people of color. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Several days later, somebody told me, your friend Oscar came in with a company of soldiers. And he's in the town of [GERMAN], which is about eight kilometers. It turned out that after we buried my friend Oscar alive, he got up at night and started wandering around. And he was fortunate enough to walk into the POW camp of American soldiers. And the German guards knew that the end was near, and they let him in. And he had pneumonia. And the medic treated him over there. And later on, he was liberated by an American company. And he stayed and worked with that company. He came to the village. And I made the decision to leave my father and my friends and go and stay with him, go to work for the company. The reason for it was that I was so impressed with the American soldiers, with the American GIs that I encountered, by their kindness, by their openness, by their honesty, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by their willingness and desire to help [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that I felt I want to go and be with those-- what I called boy giants-- these gentle giants. I felt that, for me, it was an opportunity to go and leave my old self behind. At that time, I felt very, very unclean. I felt very low. I had very low-esteem in myself. Here for six years I was told I was an untermensch. I was numb actually. I lost the ability to laugh, to smile, to love. I was callous. From a normal Jewish boy, from a decent home, I had become-- what I considered myself to be-- a cynical, callous, heartless, denigrated, debased individual, who could go through what I went through, to see what I have seen and witness what I have witnessed, and still go on, I must have lost all the dignity and all the values that were implanted in me by my grandmother and my parents. And I felt very, very low of myself. And I wanted to sever myself from that life. And I could think of all the negative things that my friends and my comrades did in the camps. And I wanted to be with these new people, with these fresh people. And I went to work for the American army. The company that I went with had a Polish Captain [POLISH] who spoke Polish. And I spoke Polish and German. He spoke Polish and English. So they made me an interpreter. Slowly I learned some of the English. I learned the GI language-- ain't you, can't you, don't you, won't you. And I became accustomed to the American culture. I was gaining weight rapidly. And our company moved out eventually to Regensburg. And while we were in Regensburg, I got an attack, what I thought was appendicitis. It turned out to be a gallbladder attack. And I was put in a German hospital. The German doctor wanted to operate on me, and I refuse to be operated by a German doctor so shortly after the war, And I said that I don't want to be operated. So he says you'll have to be on a diet. In the interim, my company moved out from Regensburg. Several weeks later, my friend Oscar came with a jeep and picked me up. And he took me where the company was stationed. It was a different company at that time. They changed. And we stayed in the estate of a baron, [GERMAN] in southern Bavaria. And the company that I worked for then was the 35th Tank Battalion Fourth Armored Third Army, General Patton's troop. As a matter of fact, about five, six weeks before General Patton was killed, he gave an inspection to our company.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember him coming into the dining room and yelling, hey, soldier, what's your occupation in this organization? Because here I was standing in an American uniform, not saluting. And I think he issued an ordinance where all displaced people working for the Americans had to have uniforms dyed black, with a patch indicating the origin of their country. OK. We have to stop there. This tape--
